results.title: It appears that Apple users are being affected by the same WebKit ...
excerpts:
## Tom's Guide's Post

* * *

[](https://web.facebook.com/Tomsguide?__cft__\[0\]=AZYDgi7JK8w34NVBlf2gJPGesFuMPPdx2ixbxMuBtchfG-Okou9OUvAAsITXR7QEdHW0kORgsv7siCuxsYW2BgdKmum3FQWsAC9oo96251j2x5OD86WbidhqvLntCX0Ena4L31xI3Xs1asd__O1qRgz9mcl5NjxpqXdKI6iWBaf0fw&__tn__=%3C%3C%2CP-R)

### [**Tom's Guide**](https://web.facebook.com/Tomsguide?__cft__\[0\]=AZYDgi7JK8w34NVBlf2gJPGesFuMPPdx2ixbxMuBtchfG-Okou9OUvAAsITXR7QEdHW0kORgsv7siCuxsYW2BgdKmum3FQWsAC9oo96251j2x5OD86WbidhqvLntCX0Ena4L31xI3Xs1asd__O1qRgz9mcl5NjxpqXdKI6iWBaf0fw&__tn__=-UC%2CP-R)

[December 16, 2025](https://web.facebook.com/Tomsguide/posts/pfbid0AnxgvArHQ17koMEv3QLQACSU5ACYpgz8ux8X6vZywXRJ7RT4Z3fw6w8R63bkemKvl?__cft__\[0\]=AZYDgi7JK8w34NVBlf2gJPGesFuMPPdx2ixbxMuBtchfG-Okou9OUvAAsITXR7QEdHW0kORgsv7siCuxsYW2BgdKmum3FQWsAC9oo96251j2x5OD86WbidhqvLntCX0Ena4L31xI3Xs1asd__O1qRgz9mcl5NjxpqXdKI6iWBaf0fw&__tn__=%2CO%2CP-R) Â·
It appears that Apple users are being affected by the same WebKit bug that affected Chrome users last week The iPhone maker has issued an emergency security update to protect users against two zero-day flaws that are being actively exploited by hackers. The attacks, which have been referred to as âsophisticatedâ in an Apple support document, are targeting specific individuals; in this case, those still running older versions of iOS instead of iOS 26.

Both flaws affect WebKit and these new fixes were issued in response to the same reported exploitation. The first vulnerability (tracked as CVE-2025-43529) is a use-after-free remote code execution flaw. Itâs exploited by processing maliciously crafted web content and was discovered by Googleâs Threat Analysis Group. The second vulnerability (tracked as CVE-2025-14174) is a memory corruption flaw that was discovered by both Googleâs Threat Analysis Group and Apple.
These bugs appear to be somewhat related to the zero-day flaw that Google just patched last week. It was tracked as 466192044 and didnât initially have a CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) umber but was considered to be a high severity flaw. That bug is now referred to as CVE-2025-14174 which is an out of bounds memory access in ANGLE â and is the same CVE number as above.

Link in the comments for the full story

results.title: Chrome extension MV3: persistent service worker die after wake up from hibernation - Stack Overflow
excerpts:

# Chrome extension MV3: persistent service worker die after wake up from hibernation

Ask Question

Asked 2 years ago

Modified 2 years ago

Viewed 1k times

1

I'm using this solution by wOxxOm [here](https://stackoverflow.
com/questions/66618136/persistent-service-worker-in-chrome-extension/) to keep the service worker alive in my chrome extension (manifest v3).

I used the offscreen solution because I use win7 so I can only use chrome v109. it works very well, but when the PC hibernate then wake up, the service worker will restart with a probability of 50%. for example if I hibernate after 10s and wake up after 10s then the SW will restart always...

This problem does not happen in my tests when I use background scripts using manifest v2 with `"persistent" : true`

Is there something I can do to make the SW persistent (not restart after hibernation) as in MV2?

I tested other solutions but none worked, for example I discovered that I can make the SW persistent using alarms too:

```
// keep SW alive for 30s
// self.serviceWorker.postMessage('test')

// this will keep SW alive for 5min then chrome will kill it, to prevent this kill we will run alarm every 4min
// we run setInterval every 20s to prevent SW sleep in the first minute
setInterval(()=>{self.serviceWorker.postMessage('test');console.log("___________postMessage every 20 seconds");},20000);

chrome.runtime.onInstalled.addListener(() => {
  chrome.alarms.create('keepAlive', { periodInMinutes: 4 });
});

chrome.alarms.onAlarm.addListener((info) => {
  if (info.name === 'keepAlive') {
    self.serviceWorker.postMessage('test');
    console.log("++++++++++++alarm every 4 min");
  }
});
```

But it did not solve the hibernation problem too. I'm out of ideas, any idea please?

is this problem solved in newer chrome versions? i have only win7 i cannot test the newer chrome versions unfortunately.

* google-chrome
* google-chrome-extension
* service-worker
* hibernation

Share

Improve this question

Follow

asked Mar 29, 2024 at 22:45

Badr Elmers

1,816 21 21 silver badges 24 24 bronze badges

10

* 2
Sounds like a bug in Chrome that is not related to the persistency trick: Chrome apparently adds the hibernation time to the internal idle timer of the service worker, so it becomes larger than 30 seconds. In other words the same will happen even without the persistency trick if you suspend the PC immediately after any `chrome` API event received by the service worker, wait for more than 30 seconds, resume the PC.
  
  woxxom
  
  – woxxom
  
  2024-03-29 23:10:03 +00:00
  
  Commented Mar 29, 2024 at 23:10
* @wOxxOm Thanks to God that i got an answer from you, i came here hoping to get an answer specially from you because you are the only faithful person i know who best understand the chrome internals. so i can do nothing except be prepared for this SW's restarts, thank you very much sir.
  
  Badr Elmers
  
  – Badr Elmers
  
  2024-03-29 23:27:13 +00:00
  
  Commented Mar 29, 2024 at 23:27
* 2
I'd simply use the first method ("bug exploit") because Chrome team apparently can't admit it's a bug (and I've pressed them quite persistently in public), so it won't be fixed for the foreseeable future.
  
  woxxom
  
  – woxxom
  
  2024-03-30 00:00:20 +00:00
  
  Commented Mar 30, 2024 at 0:00
* 1
  
  I guess, offscreen, because it's truely persistent, at least for the foreseeable future.
  
  woxxom
  
  – woxxom
  
  2024-03-30 00:33:39 +00:00
  
  Commented Mar 30, 2024 at 0:33
* 1
  
  See also [github.com/whatwg/html/issues/6759](https://github.com/whatwg/html/issues/6759) and try [scheduler.postTask](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Scheduler/postTask) .
  
  woxxom

results.title: An Abridged History of Safari Showstoppers - Webventures
excerpts:
And this doesn't account for the time it sometimes takes for bugs to get picked up. In the list below, you'll find actual show-stopping bugs that have lingered for years in WebKit's Bugzilla, or bugs that were fixed quickly but then didn't roll out with the next iOS update (or the one after that ).

### Why Safari's track record is important

Safari's track record shows a pattern: Time and time again, Apple has broken mission critical features. They didn't just break IndexedDB in 2014 , they broke it again in 2021. Twice !

And there is a second pattern: when something breaks in Safari, it often takes a few weeks or months - or even years(!)") \- before it's fixed.

Businesses and users can not rely on a single browser engine to never break. And history indicates that this is especially the case if that single browser engine is Safari.

### What about other browsers?

Every browser has bugs. But the sheer quantity of things that only fail in Safari is kind of baffling:
Number of tests which fail in exactly one browser. Lower is better. Source: [Web Platform Tests](https://wpt.fyi/results/?label=master&label=experimental&aligned "Web Platform Tests") .

Usually, you can just switch to another browser when something breaks. On iOS, you can't. Every browser on iOS is forced to use Apple's WebKit. If WebKit has a bug, every browser on iOS has that bug. There is no way to switch to a browser that is not broken (other than to switch to another device that runs another operating system).

So, in that sense, other browsers' bugs are not instant showstoppers. You can switch (without buying a new device).

Also, as explained above, Safari relies on system updates to roll out an update. This means that bug fixes often take a minimum of weeks, if not months, to ship. Other browsers on other platforms do not rely on system updates.
I have personal experience with Chrome bugs that were fixed within one or two weeks and shipped with a patch release outside their already fast 4-week release schedule.

## Confidence Gaming

A good way to see how the combination of Apple's prohibition on competing browsers and its own [underinvestment in Safari](https://infrequently.org/2021/04/progress-delayed/ "underinvestment in Safari") impacts users and developers is to ask _"if someone were trying to build an app, could they use the web?"_ . Organizations that bet on the web want to serve all users from a single, interoperable, portable codebase.

This means that [Apple's forced monopoly](https://open-web-advocacy.org/walled-gardens-report/ "Apple's forced monopoly") makes iOS showstoppers a threat to the idea of investing in the web for reaching the world's wealthiest users. And what business or service can shrug off their flagship experience not working for the boss or board directors because they all have iPhones?
 ... 
### 2023: New Wake Lock API does not work in Home Screen Web Apps

|WebKit bugs |[254545](https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=254545 "254545") |
| --- | --- |
|Reported: |2023 (Mar) |
|Fixed: |2023 (Apr) |
|Released: |\- |

> New Wake Lock API works in Safari browser but does not work when web page is added to home screen.  
> siim, [WebKit Bugzilla](https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=254545 "WebKit Bugzilla")
> 
> 

iOS 16.4 introduced the [Screen Wake Lock API](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Screen_Wake_Lock_API "Screen Wake Lock API") . Unfortunately, Apple completely forgot to enable it for web apps when they implemented it. It works fine in Safari, but breaks as soon as a user figures out how to install the web app (good thing that [only 4 out of 10 people know](https://blog.mozilla.
org/ux/2023/02/people-do-use-add-to-home-screen/#:~:text=Of%20the%20ten%20people%20we%20talked%20to%2C%20four%20were%20familiar%20with%20this%20flow "only 4 out of 10 people know") you can "Add to Homescreen" on iOS 🫠).

As you can imagine, being able to set a wake lock is especially important for games, media websites, custom video players, and document editors / storage services like [Cryptee](https://crypt.ee/ "Cryptee") <sup>[[6]]( "[6]")</sup> , that needs to encrypt and upload documents before the phone goes to sleep. You know, things that would make sense as PWAs.

The issue in WebKit was [fixed pretty quickly](https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=254545 "fixed pretty quickly") , but it didn't take long for web developers to notice that [the fix didn't land](https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=254545 "the fix didn't land") on actual devices. WebKit responded:

> I should also mention that the fix isn't in WebKit.
We added API to WebKit to allow the Safari team to fix this (and thus closed this WebKit bug report). However, the issue won't be resolved until Safari actually adopts the new WebKit API.  
> Chris Dumez, [WebKit Bugzilla](https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=254545 "WebKit Bugzilla")
> 
> 

This comment was made on May 19th 2023. The fix didn't ship in any of the iOS 16 releases, and skipped iOS 17.0, 17.1 and 17.2 as well. Developers kept asking when this would get fixed. In January 2024 (9 months after iOS 16.4 shipped the Wake Lock API), the WebKit team replied this:

> There is no issue to fix on WebKit side, therefore there is no reason to have a WebKit bugzilla bug. There is already a radar and it is already assigned to the correct team at Apple. This issue was not forgotten.  
> Chris Dumez, [WebKit Bugzilla](https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=254545 "WebKit Bugzilla")
> 
> 

Fast forward a couple of months (like, say, nine). Babies were conceived and born, iOS 18.
0 is here, and [no one at Apple Safari has bothered](https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=254545 "no one at Apple Safari has bothered") to hook up the APIs that the WebKit team provided to them in May 2023.

**18 months after the bug was filed and 16 months after it was fixed in WebKit, the Wake Lock API still does not work in installed web apps** .

> [...] remember that Cupertino's position to regulators is:
> 
> * Safari is so good that allowing other engines would only ruin a good thing
> * The web is a real substitute for native apps because Safari is so capable
> * Safari is not behind on features
> * Apple's approach "is working"
> 
> Alex Russell, [Mastodon](https://elk.zone/mastodon.social/@slightlyoff@toot.cafe/113199685627405719 "Mastodon")
> 
> 

1. "This episode of Webventures was brought to you by..." 😉 ↩
